section.title: Examples of Judgment
section.sectionRationale: Having named the intruders, Jude proves their doom from history. Three examples of past judgment, unbelieving Israel, the fallen angels, and Sodom and Gomorrah, show that God has always judged the ungodly. Then he turns back to these dreamers, who repeat the old rebellions and even slander what the archangel Michael would not.
section.thesis: God has a settled record of judging the ungodly, whether unbelieving Israel, rebel angels, or immoral cities; so the false teachers, who defile, reject authority, and slander, stand under the same certain judgment.
section.frameNote: Two movements: three historical warnings of judgment, verses 5 to 7; then the application to these dreamers, with the restraint of Michael set against their reckless slander, verses 8 to 10.

verses.text: Although you are fully aware of this, I want to remind you that after Jesus had delivered His people out of the land of Egypt, He {{apollymi|destroyed}} those who did not believe.
And the angels who did not stay within their own domain but abandoned their proper dwelling, these He has {{tereo|kept}} in eternal chains under darkness, bound for judgment on that great day.

In like manner, Sodom and Gomorrah and the cities around them, who indulged in sexual immorality and pursued {{sarx_heteros|strange flesh}}, are on display as an example of those who sustain the punishment of {{aionios|eternal}} fire.
Yet in the same way these {{enypniazomai|dreamers}} defile their bodies, reject authority, and slander glorious beings.
But even the archangel Michael, when he disputed with the devil over the body of Moses, did not presume to bring a slanderous charge against him, but said, “The Lord rebuke you!”
These men, however, {{blasphemeo|slander}} what they do not understand, and like irrational animals, they will be destroyed by the things they do instinctively.

apollymi.gloss: To destroy utterly, to ruin, to bring to eternal loss. God saved Israel out of Egypt and then destroyed the generation that refused to believe. The word warns that being rescued once is no guarantee against ruin if unbelief takes hold.

tereo.gloss: To keep, guard, hold in reserve. The same word that assured believers they are kept in Christ, verse 1, now falls on the rebel angels: God has kept them in chains for judgment. The Keeper of His people is also the keeper of the condemned.

sarx_heteros.gloss: Strange or other flesh. The phrase describes the unnatural lust of Sodom and the cities around it, a pursuit of what God never sanctioned. It marks their sin as a rebellion against the created order, not mere weakness.

aionios.gloss: Eternal, everlasting, age lasting. Sodom's ruin stands as a visible example pointing to the eternal fire that awaits the ungodly. The temporal judgment is a signpost to a final and lasting one.

enypniazomai.gloss: To dream, to be given to dreams or visions. Jude calls the false teachers dreamers, likely mocking their claim to special revelations that justify their sin. Lost in their own imaginings, they defile the body and despise authority.

blasphemeo.gloss: To slander, revile, speak abusively, blaspheme. The dreamers slander glorious beings and revile what they do not understand. Even Michael, contending with the devil himself, would not stoop to such reckless abuse, leaving judgment to the Lord.

basic.context: Jude backs up his warning with three examples everyone knew. God rescued Israel from Egypt, then destroyed those who refused to believe. Angels who left their proper place are chained in darkness for judgment. Sodom and Gomorrah were burned as a public example. The point is plain: God judges the ungodly, and these false teachers are no exception. They too defile themselves, despise authority, and recklessly slander.
basic.hermeneutics: Jude reasons from history: if God judged even His rescued people, rebel angels, and notorious cities, He will surely judge these intruders. Verse 9, where Michael refuses to slander the devil, is famously drawn from a Jewish writing about the death of Moses. Jude uses a story his readers knew to make a moral point, not to endorse everything in that book.
basic.originalLanguages: God apollymi, destroyed, the unbelieving generation. He has tereo, kept, the rebel angels in chains, the very word used for keeping believers safe, now used for keeping the guilty. Sodom pursued sarx hetera, strange flesh, under aionios, eternal, fire. The intruders are enypniazomai, dreamers, who blasphemeo, slander, what they do not know.
basic.typology: Each example is a type of final judgment. The wilderness dead, the imprisoned angels, and burning Sodom all picture the coming day when the ungodly are judged. They are, as Jude says, set forth as an example, warnings written large across history for every later generation.
basic.christ: Verse 5 says it was Jesus who delivered His people from Egypt and judged the unbelievers, a striking claim that the Son was active in Israel's history. The same Lord who saves also judges. His patience now is not weakness; the God who judged Sodom will judge all wickedness on that great day.
basic.god: God is utterly consistent in judging sin. He shows that rescue does not cancel responsibility, that even mighty angels are not beyond His justice, and that persistent, unnatural rebellion meets fire. Yet the aim of these warnings is mercy: they are given so that God's people will not follow the same path.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 14 tells of the wilderness generation who perished in unbelief. Second Peter 2 gives the same three examples of judgment. And Genesis 19 records the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, cited across Scripture as a byword for judgment.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible testifies that God judges the ungodly, from the flood to Sodom to the wilderness. Jude gathers these witnesses to warn the church and to assure them that the intruders' doom is certain, while the same Lord who judges is the one who keeps His own safe for the day when He sets everything right.

context.literary: A tight triad of examples, verses 5 to 7, each introduced to build a cumulative case, followed by the application, verses 8 to 10. Jude loves triads and vivid contrast; here the restraint of Michael, verse 9, is set sharply against the reckless slander of the dreamers, verse 10. The structure moves from proof to indictment.
context.historical: Jude draws on shared Jewish memory: the Exodus generation, the fall of angels linked in some traditions to Genesis 6, and Sodom. Verse 9 alludes to a lost ending of a Jewish work, the Assumption or Testament of Moses, which described a dispute over Moses' body. Jude's first readers would have recognized the reference at once.
context.canonical: These same examples recur in 2 Peter 2, marking a common apostolic stock of warnings. They tie Jude into the whole canon's witness that God judges rebellion, from the Torah through the prophets to the New Testament's final reckoning.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove from three well known instances that God certainly judges the ungodly, and thus to seal the doom of the intruders while warning the faithful not to presume on past deliverance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The examples describe historical judgments; the enduring lesson is prescriptive by implication: do not follow the way of unbelief, rebellion, and immorality, and leave slander and vengeance to the Lord, as Michael did.

debates.question: How should we understand Jude's use of the Assumption of Moses in verse 9?

views.label: Citing a true detail preserved in a Jewish tradition
views.case: Jude, under inspiration, affirms a genuine event, Michael's refusal to slander the devil, that had been passed down in the Testament of Moses. Origen linked the account to that book. Inspiration can affirm a true report found in an uninspired source, just as Paul quotes pagan poets, Acts 17:28, without endorsing their whole works.

views.label: Using a well known story to make a moral point
views.case: Others hold that Jude appeals to a story his readers knew, whether or not every detail is historically verifiable, in order to teach a lesson about restraint, without thereby canonizing the book it came from.

debates.bearing: Either way, Jude does not treat the Assumption of Moses as Scripture. He uses a recognized account to teach that even the mightiest angel left judgment to God, a rebuke to the dreamers who slander freely. The point is moral, and the orthodox reading keeps the book outside the canon while honoring the truth Jude draws from it.

debates.question: Who are the angels who did not stay within their own domain?

views.label: Angels who fell and were imprisoned
views.case: Jude refers to angelic beings who abandoned their proper place and are now kept in chains for judgment, echoing traditions built on Genesis 6 and reflected in 2 Peter 2:4.

views.label: A specific rebellion at the fall of Satan
views.case: Some connect it more broadly to the original angelic rebellion; the details are not spelled out, but the certainty of their judgment is.

debates.bearing: The specifics are secondary to Jude's point: if God did not spare even rebel angels but keeps them for judgment, the false teachers will not escape either.

study.originalLanguages: The verbs carry the weight. God apollymi, destroyed, the unbelieving generation He had just rescued, a warning that deliverance is no license for unbelief. He has tereo, kept, the fallen angels in eternal chains, the same verb that guards believers in verse 1, now guarding the condemned for judgment; the Keeper cuts both ways. Sodom pursued sarx hetera, strange flesh, and is displayed under aionios, eternal, fire. The intruders are enypniazomai, dreamers, drifting on their own visions, who blasphemeo, slander, glorious beings and revile what they cannot understand, unlike Michael, who left even the devil to the Lord's rebuke.

typology.type: The wilderness generation destroyed in unbelief
typology.fulfillment: A warning type of those who profess deliverance yet perish through unbelief, fulfilled in the final judgment of the ungodly, 1 Corinthians 10:1-12.

typology.type: The angels kept in chains for the great day
typology.fulfillment: A pledge that all rebellion, however exalted, faces God's certain judgment on the last day.

typology.type: Sodom and Gomorrah under eternal fire
typology.fulfillment: A visible example set forth to point to the everlasting judgment awaiting the impenitent, 2 Peter 2:6.

study.christ: Verse 5 makes the arresting claim that it was Jesus who delivered His people out of Egypt and then destroyed the unbelievers, presenting the Son as active Lord over Israel's salvation and judgment. The Lord who redeemed and judged then is the same Lord who saves and will judge now. His present patience is not indifference; the One who rained fire on Sodom will execute judgment on that great day, even as He keeps His own secure for it.
study.god: God is shown to be perfectly consistent and just. He judges unbelief among His own rescued people, He holds even fallen angels in chains, and He makes Sodom a public example of the fate of the ungodly. His justice is neither arbitrary nor forgetful. Yet these fearful warnings are set down in mercy, to keep His people from the path that leads to ruin and to assure them that evil will not have the last word.

crossReferences.note: The Exodus generation perishes in the wilderness for unbelief, the first example Jude cites.
The same triad of angels, the flood era, and Sodom used to prove God judges the ungodly and rescues the godly.
The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, cited throughout Scripture as a pattern of judgment.
Israel's wilderness judgments written as warnings for us, so let anyone who thinks he stands take heed.

study.oneStory: Scripture's long record of judgment, the wilderness, the fallen angels, the burning cities, all bears one witness: God judges the ungodly and will not be mocked. Jude marshals these witnesses to warn the church against the intruders' path and to assure believers that the same Lord who judged then, and who keeps rebels in chains, is keeping His own people safe for the great day when He makes all things right in Christ.

sermon.title: Don't Forget This!
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Jude, this message expounds Jude 1:5-7, the three historical examples of God's judgment on the unbelieving, the fallen angels, and Sodom, showing how they warn the church. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement's core.

quiz.q: Why does Jude list Israel, the fallen angels, and Sodom?

options.text: To prove from history that God certainly judges the ungodly, so the intruders will not escape.
options.note: Jude 1:5-7 builds a cumulative case that God always judges rebellion.

options.text: To show that God only judged people in the distant past.
options.note: The examples point forward to the certain judgment of the false teachers too.

options.text: To give an interesting history lesson unrelated to his warning.
options.note: The history is directly aimed at warning about present false teachers.

options.text: To remind readers of stories they had mostly forgotten.
options.note: He says they already know these things; the aim is to apply them, not merely recall them.

quiz.q: What is striking about who delivered Israel from Egypt, according to verse 5?

options.text: It was Jesus who delivered them and then destroyed the unbelievers.
options.note: The BSB text names Jesus, presenting the Son as active Lord in Israel's history.

options.text: It was an unnamed angel acting alone.
options.note: The text points to the Lord Himself, not merely an angel.

options.text: It was Moses by his own power.
options.note: Moses led, but the deliverance and judgment are attributed to the Lord.

options.text: It was the Father, with no role for the Son.
options.note: The verse specifically highlights the Son's involvement in the deliverance.

quiz.q: What lesson does Michael's dispute with the devil teach in verse 9?

options.text: Even the mightiest angel left judgment to the Lord rather than hurling slander.
options.note: Jude 1:9 sets Michael's restraint against the dreamers' reckless slander.

options.text: That believers should try to argue with the devil directly.
options.note: The point is restraint and deferring to the Lord, not seeking such disputes.

options.text: That Michael was weaker than the devil.
options.note: It shows humility and reverence, not weakness; he said, The Lord rebuke you.

options.text: That angels never confront evil at all.
options.note: Michael did contend, but without slander, leaving the rebuke to God.

quiz.q: How should we understand Jude's use of the account about Moses' body?

options.text: He draws a true moral point from a known Jewish tradition without treating that book as Scripture.
options.note: Jude affirms the lesson of restraint without canonizing the Assumption of Moses.

options.text: He proves that the Assumption of Moses is inspired Scripture.
options.note: Using a source is not endorsing it as Scripture; Paul likewise quoted pagan poets.

options.text: He is simply mistaken and included a legend by accident.
options.note: Jude uses the reference deliberately and under inspiration to teach a real lesson.

options.text: He refers to a story his readers knew to make his point vivid.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but the fuller point is that he affirms the moral truth it carries.

quiz.q: How does the word kept, used of the angels, connect to the rest of Jude?

options.text: The same God who keeps believers safe also keeps rebels in chains for judgment.
options.note: Tereo runs through Jude, cutting both ways: security for the saints, reservation for the guilty.

options.text: It shows God has lost track of the fallen angels.
options.note: The opposite: they are securely held for the day of judgment.

options.text: It means the angels are being protected for their own good.
options.note: They are kept for judgment, not preserved for blessing.

options.text: It is an unrelated use of a common word.
options.note: It is a deliberate keyword linking the fate of the wicked to the safety of the saved.

questions: God judged a generation He had rescued from Egypt. How does this warn us against presuming that past blessing excuses present unbelief?
The false teachers were dreamers who defiled the body and despised authority. Where are you tempted to follow your own imaginings rather than submit to God's revealed truth?
Michael refused to slander even the devil, leaving the rebuke to the Lord. How can you practice that restraint in your own conflicts and disagreements?
These sober examples of judgment were written to keep God's people from the same path. How does a clear view of God's justice help you take sin seriously?
The same Lord who judged Sodom keeps His own people safe for the great day. How does holding both truths together, His justice and His keeping, shape the way you live?

translationNotes.note: The BSB reads Jesus, with a footnote that some manuscripts read the Lord. Either reading points to the Lord's own action in delivering and judging Israel.
Where the BSB prints a dash after abandoned their proper dwelling, it has been rendered as a comma per house style. Eternal chains under darkness pictures secure imprisonment awaiting judgment.
Strange flesh renders sarx hetera, describing the unnatural desire of Sodom; the cities stand as a public example of eternal fire.
This alludes to a Jewish account, attributed by Origen to the Assumption or Testament of Moses, describing a dispute over Moses' body; Jude cites it for its moral lesson, not as Scripture.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Jude 1:5-10. The dash in verse 6 has been rendered as a comma per house style. Verse 5 follows the BSB reading Jesus, noting the variant the Lord in the footnote.
